title: QUIC RFC 9000 MAX_DATA and MAX_STREAM_DATA Runtime Credit Publication Architecture
purpose: Describe the bounded active-runtime path that publishes protected MAX_DATA and MAX_STREAM_DATA frames after receive-side application reads make additional connection and stream credit available.
design_summary: The receive-side stream bookkeeping raises the connection and stream receive limits only after application bytes are actually consumed. `QuicStream` converts those non-default credit updates into an internal flow-control-credit event, and the active connection runtime protects each resulting MAX_DATA or MAX_STREAM_DATA payload as an application-data packet through the existing sender/recovery path. Empty reads and post-terminal read attempts do not publish new credit, while the most recent protected MAX_* packets remain ordinary retransmittable application-data packets for the sender/recovery ledger.
data_and_state_considerations: Credit ownership remains split between `QuicConnectionStreamState`, which computes the absolute connection and stream limits, and `QuicConnectionRuntime`, which serializes and protects those frame payloads. The slice does not add a dynamic RTT-based autotuning policy, a batching scheduler, public flow-control APIs, or broad sender/recovery rewrites.
edge_cases_and_constraints: MAX_DATA must carry the current connection maximum data value computed after bytes are consumed.
MAX_STREAM_DATA must carry the current absolute stream offset and matching stream ID.
No protected MAX_DATA or MAX_STREAM_DATA packet may be emitted when no bytes were consumed.
After a stream reaches a terminal receive state, the runtime must not emit additional MAX_STREAM_DATA frames for that stream.
The protected MAX_* packets must remain visible to the application-data sender/recovery ledger for ordinary loss repair.
risks: Treating inbound peer MAX frames as proof of outbound receiver credit publication would leave the runtime packet path unproven.
Conflating the existing consume-bytes credit bump with a dynamic RTT-based autotuning policy would overstate the implemented flow-control behavior.
Publishing credit on empty reads would create avoidable ack-eliciting traffic without making additional peer sends valid.

supplemental_sections.heading: Scope
supplemental_sections.content: This design covers only active-runtime protected MAX_DATA and MAX_STREAM_DATA publication after supported application reads consume receive-side bytes, plus the no-consumed-bytes and terminal-stream suppression branches. It does not claim receiver-side dynamic autotuning, opportunistic packet coalescing, 0-RTT, key update, migration, public API changes, or a broader send scheduler.
